115.11: wruh ta juga ṅhulun wĕkasnya, paṅawaśa nikaṅ durnimitta katon kādbhuta niyatanya. Kinālakĕn i ṅhulun mata ya, krodha tĕkapnya nirādarâwamāna, pinintonan i ṅhulun ta ya rūpa mahābhīṣaṇa, salwir nikaṅ kātara kādbhuta pinakālakĕn i ṅhulun. Aṅālakĕn, kinālakĕn, to give time, wait? pinakālakĕn (pf), to make time for? Unclear too: BK 1.11: (Dwārawatī) laṅgĕṅ kapweki ramyanya tuwi paḍa mahāmūlya kālanya makweh. citations.text: 11.95: pĕjah pinarasraya (read pinara#jaya?) kinalana aneṅ wĕṅi. Uncertain: RL 7.20: de nipun tani desa kinalaneṅ (kinala neṅ? kinalan eṅ?) Maja-Pahit. It seems possible that kinala and kinalan are derivatives from hala, with a contraction of kahala to kāla, so that kinala = kinahala and kinalan = kinahalan (see under hala I). definition: Kalaṅ and tuha kalaṅ are passim mentioned in inscriptions (between 804 and 943 AD) as receiving gifts after taking part in the ceremony of manusuk sīma. Kalaṅ are probably a group of people with a special occupation (wood-working, building? cf kalaṅ maṅuwu?). For ``head of the kalaṅs'' as a high official (paṅeran, bupati, tumĕṅguṅ) in the 17th century see B. Schrieke, Indonesian Sociological Studies II (1957), Index s.v. kalaṅ.

sub_entries.definition: a group of royal servants or functionaries under a juru paṅalasan. In kidungs we find the paṅalasan carrying messages or executing orders from the king, conducting guests into his presence etc. Derivation uncertain, but Berg's hypothesis that paṅalasan is derived from kalasā (he who is given or entitled to a kalasā) seems the most likely. 7.1: sampun madandan atĕhĕr lumaris kamantyan. The form is uncertain. Jb. gives some examples under manti and some under kamantyan. Is there a connection with anti? The form kama-an is unknown to me, although kama-ĕn is known, but the function does not seem to fit here. Pigeaud was apparently thinking of anti when he translated i kamantyan with ``provisionally'' (Nag 18.8), but this does not make sense in the other cases. It is used in most texts, but is not found in AW, AWj, TK, TP, KK, KHWj, Mal, Td, Tk. Tan kamantyan (in KS and RL) seems to be taken as meaning ``let there be told'':

4.4: atalaṅkup aṅagĕm kayu kanaka supattra-lepana. Is it sandalwood (cf Skt kanaka)? Is it the same as kayu mas (see under kayu)? The question is also relevant with regard to the meaning of kanaka drawa and kanaka cūrṇa. In some places ``liquid gold, gold paint'' (kanaka drawa) and ``gold powder, gold dust'' (kanaka cūrṇa) seem to be the correct meanings. But elsewhere, when the context (jĕnu, wĕḍak, gandha) points to a cosmetic unguent, face-powder or perfume, kanaka may mean bdellium (a fragrant gum resin, used as incense and in perfumes) or sandalwood (yellow and fragrant). Cf Skt cūrṇa, aromatic powder, pounded sandal.

(Witaraga): kabuyutan, lĕwas sakanya sisih ariñcuṅ pan meh katuṅkĕban katuwwan iṅ karayap lukut. Bal.com. renders karamayan amilĕt (Sut 9.7) with: winilĕt iṅ karayap. Karayap and karayapan seem to be the same as karamayan, q.v. In RPWj, Wita#raga and bal. com. it is used as a noun, in BK and Td as an adjective. Although ``eaten by termites'' -- from rayap, termite (ModJ, not found in OJ) -- would give sense, it does not fit in RPWj.

definition: (The word presents many difficulties and it is not clear whether a base-word sula has to be admitted. The following interpretations try to do justice to the various ways in which it occurs, but are given with all proper reserves.) Kasula: as an alternative, or else, or better; -- which is preferable --, -- which seems the only way left-; in an irrevocable or desperate decision (lĕhĕṅ pĕjah pwa kasula), in an unjustifiable decision, going too far (lalu kasula)

sub_entries.definition: having decided that death is preferable

72.15: akilatba#hu akĕmĕr apus punḍutan agĕlaṅ goṅsor. As the kĕmĕr is often mentioned alongside the kĕṇḍit (q.v.), we might assume that it is not the same, but a closely related article of attire. It is derived from Persian kamar, cf. Eng. ``cummerbund'', a sash or girdle. Mod. Jav. ĕmĕr is ``a belt of metal, principally worn by women'', while Malay kĕmar is (Wilk.) ``clasp of girdle;--pĕnding, (Pal.) mĕr; (Jav.) ĕmĕr and Pal. mĕr is ``waist-buckle or belt, esp. of gold or silver''. Malay pĕndiṅ is then (Wilk.): ``Embossed plate of metal covering a clasp through which a girdle ... passes; worn as an ornament in front of the body ... In shape roughly elliptical ... '', while Jav. is ``metal girdle, with a plate in front'' (GR). Hence the combination kĕmĕr kĕṇḍit perhaps means ``a metal buckle and girdle'', kĕmĕr being the buckle, and kĕṇḍit at the same time having a more general meaning. Kĕmĕr is a specifically kidung word, and thus the combination also, while kĕṇḍit is also found in kakawins. (Was there an earlier equivalent of kĕmĕr?). Some examples (see also under kĕṇḍit) suggest that there may have been a magical significance for arousing love or warding off evil.

sub_entries.definition: to take one's own life (perhaps especially by hanging)

citations.text: 31.27: ya tan pĕjaha pwa si Kīcaka denta, akĕṇḍatâlabuh er ṅhulun;

16.13: keraṅan taṅ stri luṅâlabuh paruṅ lyan akĕnḍat anâjar-ajara. In ModJ. ṅĕṇḍat is used for all sorts of suicide -- hanging, drowning or jumping over cliffs -- but for hanging in particular there exists the term ṅĕṇḍat tali. The examples above, however, show that akĕṇḍat is distinct from both alabuh er and alabuh paruṅ. The ModJ. meaning of ``to break off'' appears to be absent.

definition: (Skt alone, mere, excluding others; isolated, abstract, absolute; simple, pure; entire, whole; adv. (-am) only, merely; doctrine of the absolute unity) only, merely, simply, purely, exclusively, nothing but. It occurs after the word qualified (e.g. putih kewala), or before. It also appears to be used predicatively, and sometimes somewhat pregnantly: ``(occupying os. with, devoting os. to) completely and utterly, purely and solely; (keeping os.) free of all other; undiluted, concentrated''.

sub_entries.definition: to concentrate on; to seize by force, overwhelm

(VIb:) tan tama atah i saṅ hyaṅ dharma, kewala saṅ hyaṅ dharma pramāṇa i sadṛwya hajinya kabeh. These maṅilala dṛbya haji are mentioned in many charters; to them belong:

74.285 f. (903) B.4: maṅilala drabya haji kabeh kriṅ paḍam apuy pamanikan maniga lwa malaṇḍaṅ mahuri (sic) makalaṅkaṅ tapa haji airhaji widu maṅiduṅ tuha paḍahi kĕḍi walyan paranakan sambal sumbul watak i dalam siṅgah pamṛṣi hulun haji ityewamādi, tan tamā irikanaṅ wanua ri Tĕlaṅ. Sometimes part of the dṛwya haji is freed: ibid. B.10: kĕnāna ikana sakalĕwihnya de saṅ maṅilala soddharaha(d)i; kunaṅ ikanaṅ mañambul, maṅlakha ... kapua ya tribhāgan, sabhāga umarā riṅ maṅilala drabya haji, sabhāga umarā riṅ dharma, sabhāga umarā riṅ makĕmitan dharma. In particular the maṅhuri maṅilala are mentioned (in opp. to those maṅhuri who have no right to dṛwya haji?):

3.197a: mrak ikâñuwoṅ sumaṅganîṅ kokila. Judging from the call (anunuhu or aṅalik-alik), the cuculus orientalis is meant -- the male called tuhu and the female kolik in modern Java, where their call is considered as ominous. Is it the same as the walik, q.v.? It appears to differ from the taḍah-asih, q.v. (Cuculus flavus) and the cucur, q.v. (the male?). The kokila is not mentioned as weeping for the moon, but the walik does. In Tk 4.73b and ff. it is also called kuwoṅ, q.v.

96.7: ikaṅ bala rākṣasânututi ghūrṇita saha bala-kośawāhana;
19.7: suśakti subala saha kośawāhana pĕpĕk; cf Nag 83.1: bhṛtya mwaṅ kośa len wāhana gaja turagâdinya (here wāhana refers to gaja ... );

2.15: aṅanakĕn sañjata kosa-wahana niṅ ajurit. We have here a frequent use of kośa(wāhana), which as such seems to be unknown in Skt. Kośa is here: everything the troops need, stores, supplies. Kośawāhana (not to be taken as dwandwa) is then: everything which carries supplies for the troops (wagons, horses, etc), impedimenta, wagon-train. When preceded by bala it seems to be either: supplies, vehicles for the troops (HW 32.1;  AWj 42.3; Sut 96.7), or (bala + kośawāhana): troops and their supplies, fully equipped troops (GK 2.10). Semantically it comes close to kĕkĕs (q.v.), aṅĕkĕs, pakĕkĕs (equipment).
31b.3: wwaṅ koṣṭan, edan, ayan.

definition: (Skt going, course; regular (uninterrupted) progress, order, series, succession; method, manner, custom, rule sanctioned by tradition). See also abhicāruka-, akṣara-, apa-, bala-, bhāwa-, ceṭikā-, dānawendra-, dāsī-, dewatā-, jaga-, jatu-, mantri-, nārī-, pañcaśīla-, pāpa-, parā-, pari-, paricārikā-, pati-, pekayodha-, priya-, pūjā-, rāja-, rūpa-, saji-, śīla-, śiṣya-, snāna-, stuti-, sukanyā-, surawara-, swa-, wali-, waṅśa-, widhi-, wi#dyā-, wīrayodha-, wiwāha-, yathā-, yoga-. 1. course, order 2. how something is or happens, state, condition 3. conduct, behaviour, way of acting; esp. right way, traditional way, custom 4. thereupon, then; duly, in due time

definition: , app. the base-words in the problematic sakurasa, sakura of the following passages:

citations.text: 15.4: solur lulut sahati lĕṅlĕṅ aluṅluṅan kūṅ / saśrī sakĕmbaṅ alaṅö sakurā sawīja;

63.13: ... ikaṅ wwaṅ prasama masö / makamukha para taṇḍa swastya prārtha (one short syllable lacking) sakura. Kurasa and kura could perhaps be the part of a plant (root? stem?), and then sakurasa (sakura): ``of the same kurasa (kura)'', used for a relationship. Bal. com. uses sakura (bawa) to render sanak dharma (adopted child?) in RL 7.157: kaṅkĕn sanak darmâji (bal. com.: inaku sakurabawa (?) olih saṅ prabu; see KBW s.v. sakura).

Skt mūlamantra) ``oṃ hrāṃ hrīṃ saḥ paramaśiwādityāya namaḥ'', proclaiming the identity of Śiwa and the Sun, used in the sūryasewana (cf Goris, diss. p. 11, and Hooykaas, Sūryasewana). Goris (diss. p. 89) quotes the Bhuwanakośa: ikaṅ mudra ya lĕwih sakeṅ arcana, ikaṅ mantra lĕwih sakeṅ mudra, ikaṅ kūṭa lĕwih sakiṅ mantra kabeh, praṇawakūṭaṃ, kūṭānām praṇawam param.
